SaaS: Microsoft 365, Dynamics 365.
memory_hooks: IaaS = Hosting. PaaS = Building. SaaS = Consuming.

notes.topic_title: Describe the core architectural components of Azure
notes.summary: Azure's architecture is built on Regions containing datacenters. To protect against datacenter failures, regions offer multiple Availability Zones. Resources are organized logically: Resources belong to Resource Groups, which belong to Subscriptions, which can be governed by Management Groups.
important_facts: Deleting a resource group permanently deletes all resources within it.
A region contains 3 Availability Zones (when supported).
Management groups can nest up to six levels deep to apply enterprise-wide governance.
examples: Deploying VMs in Zone 1, Zone 2, and Zone 3 of East US to survive a power outage in Zone 1.
Using a Management Group to enforce a policy across 50 different departmental subscriptions.
memory_hooks: Hierarchy: Management Group > Subscription > Resource Group > Resource.

notes.topic_title: Describe Azure compute, networking, and storage services
notes.summary: Compute includes VMs (IaaS), VMSS for scaling, ACI for simple containers, and Functions for serverless code. Networking utilizes VNets for isolation, Peering to connect VNets, and ExpressRoute for private on-prem connectivity. Storage offers Blob (objects), File (SMB), and tiers (Hot, Cool, Archive) balanced by redundancy options like GRS.
key_concepts: Storage tiers (Hot, Cool, Archive) and redundancy (LRS, ZRS, GRS)
important_facts: ExpressRoute never uses the public internet.
Archive tier data takes hours to retrieve (rehydration).
Azure File Sync caches cloud files onto an on-premises Windows server.
examples: Using VMSS to auto-add web servers during a sale.
Using AzCopy to script a massive data upload to Blob storage.
memory_hooks: Hot = Now, Cool = Later, Archive = Rarely. LRS = 1 datacenter, ZRS = 3 datacenters.

notes.topic_title: Describe Azure identity, access, and security
notes.summary: Microsoft Entra ID manages identity (authentication) and access (authorization via RBAC). Conditional Access applies intelligent rules (like requiring MFA based on location). Zero Trust mandates explicit verification for everything. Defender for Cloud provides posture management.
key_concepts: Zero Trust and Defense in Depth
important_facts: RBAC controls WHAT you can do to a resource; Conditional Access controls the CONDITIONS under which you log in.
Defense in depth uses multiple layers of security to protect data at the core.
examples: Enforcing MFA for remote workers (Conditional Access).
Giving a developer the 'Virtual Machine Contributor' role (RBAC).
memory_hooks: Zero Trust = Trust nobody, verify everybody. Entra External ID = Customers.

notes.summary: Azure Storage provides scalable, secure cloud storage. General Purpose v2 (GPv2) is the standard account type. Azure Files supports SMB protocols for lift-and-shift of legacy file servers. Access tiers (Hot, Cool, Archive) allow you to balance storage costs against retrieval costs based on how often data is accessed.
important_facts: GPv2 accounts support all data types: Blobs, Files, Queues, and Tables.
Archive tier has the lowest storage cost but the highest retrieval cost and longest retrieval time.
examples: Using Azure Files to replace an old on-premises Windows File Server.
Moving 10-year legal backups to the Archive tier to save money.
memory_hooks: Hot = Fast/Expensive storage. Archive = Slow/Cheap storage.

notes.topic_title: Describe Azure identity, access, and security
notes.summary: Identity in Azure is managed by Microsoft Entra ID. Single Sign-On (SSO) streamlines user experience. For administrative security, Privileged Identity Management (PIM) ensures users only have powerful roles exactly when they need them (Just-In-Time access).
important_facts: SSO reduces the number of passwords users need to remember, thereby increasing security.
PIM requires users to justify their need for admin rights and automatically revokes them after a set time.
examples: Logging into a laptop and automatically being authenticated to Outlook and HR systems (SSO).
Requesting 2-hour Contributor access to restart a crashed server (PIM).

notes.summary: Managing costs requires understanding the difference between the Pricing Calculator (estimating new deployments) and the TCO Calculator (estimating on-prem migration savings). Utilizing reservations and tagging resources heavily influences financial efficiency.
important_facts: Tags are not inherited by default from resource groups to resources.
Reservations offer discounts for 1 or 3-year commitments.
examples: Using tags like 'Environment: Production' to track spending per environment.
Buying a 3-year reservation for an always-on SQL server to reduce compute costs.
memory_hooks: TCO = Total Cost of OLD (migrating on-prem). Pricing Calc = New stuff.

notes.summary: Governance is enforced via Azure Policy (rules) and Resource Locks (prevent deletion/modification). Purview governs data, and Arc extends ARM to hybrid environments. Deployments are managed using ARM templates or Bicep (declarative infrastructure as code).
key_concepts: Microsoft Purview and Service Trust Portal
Azure Arc, ARM templates, and Bicep
important_facts: Locks override RBAC permissions. A 'CanNotDelete' lock stops an Owner from deleting a resource.
Bicep is a simpler, cleaner alternative to JSON for ARM templates.
Service Trust Portal is where you find Microsoft's audit reports.
examples: Policy preventing deployment outside of Europe.
Using Azure Arc to manage AWS servers in the Azure Portal.
memory_hooks: Policy = Compliant Configuration. Locks = Prevent Deletion. RBAC = Who has access.

notes.summary: Azure Monitor collects and analyzes telemetry using Log Analytics and Application Insights. Azure Service Health reports on platform-wide outages, while Azure Advisor acts as a cloud consultant, providing tips on cost, security, reliability, performance, and operations.
important_facts: App Insights focuses on code-level issues for developers.
Advisor is free and provides personalized best-practice recommendations.
examples: Checking Service Health during a regional outage.
Writing a KQL query in Log Analytics to find failed logins across 100 VMs.
memory_hooks: Monitor = Performance. Service Health = Platform Outages. Advisor = Recommendations.

notes.topic_title: Describe features and tools for managing and deploying Azure resources
notes.summary: Azure offers various management interfaces. The Azure portal provides a GUI, while Cloud Shell provides a browser-based CLI (supporting Bash and PowerShell). For repeatable, error-free deployments, Infrastructure as Code is utilized via ARM templates or Bicep.
key_concepts: Azure Portal, Mobile App, Cloud Shell
important_facts: ARM templates are declarative: you specify the desired end-state, not the steps to get there.
Cloud Shell requires an Azure Storage account to persist files across sessions.
examples: Restarting a VM from an airport using the Azure Mobile App.
Using a Bicep template to deploy a Development and Production environment exactly the same way.
memory_hooks: Imperative = How to do it (Scripts). Declarative = What it should look like (Templates).

notes.summary: Azure Monitor is a comprehensive telemetry platform. It collects raw metrics and routes logs using Diagnostic Settings. Log Analytics uses KQL for deep log querying. Application Insights provides developers with code-level diagnostics, while Alerts provide proactive notifications.
important_facts: Without configuring Diagnostic Settings, detailed resource logs are not retained long-term.
Alerts can be configured to trigger automated actions (like a webhook) when a threshold is met.
examples: Sending an email alert when CPU usage > 90%.
Using App Insights to figure out why a 'Submit Order' button is slow.
memory_hooks: Metrics = Numbers. Logs = Events. KQL = The search engine for Logs.

notes.topic_title: Describe features and tools in Azure for governance and compliance
notes.summary: Governance ensures organizations stay compliant and secure. Policy Initiatives group related rules. Resource Locks prevent accidental deletion and override Owner permissions. Purview acts as an enterprise-wide map for sensitive data classification.
important_facts: A CanNotDelete lock defeats an Owner's permission to delete.
Azure Blueprints is a legacy concept replaced by Azure Landing Zones and Policy Initiatives.
examples: Grouping 10 different security policies into one 'PCI-DSS Initiative'.
Using Purview to find out where credit card numbers are stored across Azure and AWS.
memory_hooks: Purview = Pure View of all your data.

notes.summary: Cost optimization is critical. Budgets and alerts prevent overspending. Azure Hybrid Benefit leverages existing on-prem licenses to save money, while Spot VMs offer massive discounts for interruptible workloads.
important_facts: Reservations discount hardware. Hybrid Benefit discounts software.
Spot VMs can be evicted at any time; never use them for critical databases.
examples: Running a machine learning batch job on Spot VMs to save 80%.
Bringing an on-prem Windows Server license to Azure using Hybrid Benefit.
memory_hooks: Spot VMs = Cheap but risky. Reservations = Commitment but safe.
